title: Khunrath's Confessio: The Triune Chaos and the Spirit of the Lord in Salt
subtitle: Henry Khunrath's 'A Naturall Chymicall Symbolum' — the confession concerning the Universal Chaos of Alchemy, the Three-in-One Mercury, and the Ruach Elohim incorporate in Salt

summary.one_line: Khunrath's 1597 'Naturall Chymicall Symbolum' — the confession that the Ruach Elohim (Spirit of the Lord) is become a corporal Salt, the triune Azoth as Body, Soul, and Spirit, and the Amphitheatru...
summary.scope: Khunrath's Confessio from MS. Ashmole 1459, covering the triune nature of the Philosopher's Mercury (Body, Soul, Spirit as one Azoth), the incarnation of Ruach Elohim in the 'Virgin Womb of the Earth,' the relation of the Chaos to the Macrocosmic and Microcosmic Trinity, and the alchemical-Cabalistic schema of the Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae.

sources.label: Khunrath's Confessio — A Naturall Chymicall Symbolum from MS. Ashmole 1459, pp. 99-106

body.text: This is the true philosophicall doctrine of the philosophers Mercury: That Three is One generall Chaos, Three in essence, namely Body, Soule, & Spiritt; & these Three Essences are had in One substance or thing & neere at hand. And neither is their Substance changed one into the other, nor is the Essence dissolved in the Substance, or divided in ye Subject. There is one Essence of the Body one other of ye Spirit, one other of the Soule; But ye Body, Soule, & Spirit are one thing, wherein all the three are together equally necessarily present at the same time. Like as ye Body even such is the Spirit, & such is the Soule. For the Body is not made by the Arte of Man, nor is the Spirit made by the Arte of Man, neither is the Soule made by the Arte of Man. So then it is one Body, not three Bodies, one Spirit, not three Spirits, one Soule not three Soules. Amongst these all three Chaos'ses wch the Philosophers call their Magnesia, none is first, none last, none more necessary naturally; But all three equally subsist, are wth ye other naturally in the Chaos, and are alike Coequall. So yt may be said of all universal Mercury, it is three in one, and one in three.

But it is further necessary in the Catholike Chymicall Arte, yt we truly believe (ye know directly) & stedfastly hold that Ruach Elohim the Spirit of ye Lord wch in Gen: 1.2. did move upon the Water, is really become a body. That Spirit of the Lord was OR preceeding of the Essence of the Spirit of the Lord (wch in ye beginning of the world was wth the Lord) wch Gen: 1,2 moved upon the waters, wch Wisd: 1,7 filled the whole world, and wch Wisd: 11,26 is in all, even in ye most inward & sacredest Virgin wombe and centre of the Earth, the most misticall broodie Mother of the greate world, bodily it is become a Corporall Salt of wisdome.

Thus Magnesia its owne Sone, clenseth it from ye impure superfluous Nature. Which when OR hath suffered Chymically and naturally as aforesaid (yea about ye universall chimicall godly magick & Christian cabalisticall salvacion of man as I have tought in ye 4 figures of my Amphitheatre of Eternal Wisdome), for yt safety of ye greate world & is by natural opperacion buried & descended into Hell, in the third of his oft mencioned labours, or worke, he shall rise from death without impuritie or corruptableness, Ascende into the heaven of his more than full perfection, & in full brightness sitteth at the right power and strength of the great world, his potent father in a Christaline transparentness & Carbuncle like redness, wth out corruptableness compleat in Body, Soule and Spirit, like a perfect Quintessence. From hence cometh OR Compleate by naturall opperacion to Judge both the litle and the greate world, sound & sick.

Khunrath's triune division of the Philosopher's Mercury into Body, Soul, and Spirit — coequal and coinherent — is the alchemical formulation of what the Tantraloka (4.48-65) calls the trikaya: the three bodies of the absolute (parama-shiva) as nishkala (without parts), sakala-nishkala (with and without parts), and sakala (with parts). The Body (sthula-sharira) corresponds to the gross Mercury — the material chaos; the Soul (jivatman) is the Mercury of life — the 'sensitive' or 'astral' Mercury; the Spirit (atman) is the volatile Mercury — the unmanifest essence. That 'none is first, none last, none more necessary' reflects the Tantric teaching of the non-hierarchical interpenetration of the three bodies in the state of sahaja (co-emergence). In Steiner's anthroposophy, this triunity corresponds to the threefold nature of the human being: the physical body (the 'Body' of Khunrath's Mercury), the etheric body (the 'Soul' — the life-principle), and the astral body (the 'Spirit' — the consciousness-principle). Khunrath's insistence that these three are 'one thing' not three separate things is the same doctrine that the Layayoga teaches about the three nadis (ida, pingala, susumna): they are distinct currents but one system of subtle energy.

Khunrath's bold claim that Ruach Elohim — the Spirit of God that moved upon the face of the waters in Genesis — has 'become a Corporall Salt' in the 'most inward & sacredest Virgin wombe and centre of the Earth' is the alchemical statement of what the Sat-cakra-nirupana describes as the kundalini-shakti sleeping in the muladhara chakra, the 'earth-centre' of the subtle body. The 'Virgin wombe' is the triangular yoni of the muladhara where the coiled serpent resides; the 'Salt of wisdome' is the latent consciousness that, when 'fixed' and 'corporified,' becomes the Philosopher's Stone — the elixir of both personal and cosmic redemption. The PGM calls this the 'holy spirit that descends into the initiate' during the theurgic baptism (PGM IV.475-829). In Steiner's account, the Ruach Elohim that 'brooded over the waters' is the Macrocosmic Christ — the creative word that, in the alchemical process of the human being, becomes 'salt' (the fixed physical body) that must be spiritualized again through the resurrection of the 'carbuncle' (the transformed etheric heart).

Khunrath's reference to his 'Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae' and its four figures — which depict the ladder of ascent from the elemental world to the Cabalistic sephiroth — is the visual representation of the daimonic path of initiation. The Amphitheatre shows the seven planetary spheres (the 'seven days of creation') through which the soul must pass to reach the 'Carbuncle-like redness' of the perfected Stone, which is the eighth — the ogdoad of the Hermetic regeneration (CH XIII). The 'descent into Hell' that OR (the spirit) must undergo before its resurrection is the katabasis — the 'night sea journey' — that every initiate must make to contact the daimon in the underworld. This is the same process that the PGM describes in the 'Mithras Liturgy' (PGM IV.475-829): the descent into the underworld of the elements, the vision of the seven planetary gatekeepers, and the final ascent to the 'fiery god' that is the daimonic guide. In Tantric terms, this is the purification of the seven chakras that must be 'opened' through the serpent's ascent, each 'gate' guarded by its own tattvic power, until the 'carbuncle' of the sahasrara — the thousand-petalled lotus of crystalline light — is attained.

gaps: Khunrath's 'Philosophicall short songe' and the Cabalistic dimensions of the Amphitheatrum need deeper treatment

issues.title: Passages are extracts, not full verbatim text
issues.description: The Confessio is long. Key sections on the triune Mercury, Ruach Elohim, and the Amphitheatrum are extracted. Full text with line references needed.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Khunrath's Confessio. Six passages cover the triune Mercury, Ruach Elohim in Salt, and the Amphitheatrum, with commentary connecting to the trikaya doctrine, kundalini in muladhara, and daimonic katabasis.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Khunrath's Confessio
